one_line_takeaway: Representation Unlearning replaces costly parameter-level machine unlearning with a lightweight, information-bottleneck-trained transformation over penultimate-layer representations, achieving near-Retraining forgetting/utility with up to 757x speed-ups (including a Neural-Collapse-based zero-shot variant needing no retain data), at the acknowledged cost of leaving the underlying encoder weights -- and thus the raw representation's latent information -- unchanged.
key_finding: On Class Unlearning (Table 1), Representation Unlearning (Rep. Unl.) attains Test Ar/Test Af/Test CE of 93.5/1.9/0.36 on CIFAR-10, 73.4/0.2/0.88 on CIFAR-100, and 58.7/0.6/1.80 on Tiny ImageNet, beating parameter-centric baselines on utility retention while approaching near-perfect forgetting; e.g., Bad Teacher only reaches 67.2% Ar on CIFAR-100 and SCRUB only 48.9% Ar on Tiny ImageNet, versus the Retraining gold standard's 74.4% and 59.7% respectively. In the harder zero-shot class-unlearning setting, Rep. Unl. ZS reaches 93.2/4.0/0.42 (CIFAR-10), 69.3/0.2/0.97 (CIFAR-100), and 51.6/0.5/1.98 (Tiny ImageNet), while zero-shot baselines catastrophically degrade utility (EMMN collapses to 1.2% Ar on CIFAR-100 and 0.5% on Tiny ImageNet). On Random Unlearning (Table 2), Rep. Unl. achieves Train Ar/RMIA/Test CE of 100.0/57.7/0.14 (CIFAR-10), 99.9/70.9/0.69 (CIFAR-100), and 100.0/75.6/1.13 (Tiny ImageNet), with RMIA values close to the Retraining baseline (54.2/68.7/73.0 respectively) while retaining near-perfect training accuracy; SCRUB by contrast collapses to 54.5% Ar on Tiny ImageNet. Computationally (Fig. 4), on Tiny ImageNet Rep. Unl. ZS delivers a 757x speed-up over full retraining and Rep. Unl. (standard) delivers 233x, both far outpacing every other baseline (e.g., SISA 5.9x, SCRUB not separately labeled but sub-20x range, EMMN/GKT below 1x i.e. slower than retraining). Inference-time overhead of the added transformation layer is reported as microsecond-scale (0.59 microseconds per forward pass for a 1-layer MLP on CIFAR-10, 1.09 microseconds on Tiny ImageNet).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Representation Unlearning, a machine-unlearning framework that performs forgetting entirely in a model's representation space rather than by modifying model parameters. It learns a lightweight transformation f_phi (a linear map or shallow MLP) applied to the penultimate-layer activations Z, trained with an information-bottleneck objective: maximize mutual information between the transformed representation Z' and the retain data Dr while suppressing mutual information between Z' and the forget data Df. Because the two mutual-information terms (I(Z'; Z | Xr) and I(Z'; Xf)) are intractable, the authors derive variational upper bounds (Appendix A.1-A.5) that reduce, under Gaussian parameterizations, to simple mean-squared-error losses (Lr in Eq. 5, Lf in Eq. 17), combined as L = Lr + beta*Lf with beta=1e-3. They instantiate this in two regimes: (i) a standard setting with access to both retain and forget data, and (ii) a zero-shot setting with access only to the forget set, which they make tractable by invoking the Neural Collapse hypothesis (Papyan et al., 2020) to approximate per-class representation centroids as the classifier's own weight vectors wc, letting the model synthesize 'pseudo-targets' for retain and forget losses (Eq. 10, Eq. 20) without needing real retain-data samples.
threat_model: The paper's own stated threat model (Limitations, Sec. 5) is black-box / API-access: the method assumes an adversary or auditor only observes the model's exposed outputs / representations Z' after the transformation is applied, not the underlying encoder weights. The authors explicitly note this is a limitation, not a strength: because the encoder parameters themselves are left unchanged, the raw (untransformed) representation Z still encodes information about the forget set, so the approach is 'vulnerable to white-box recovery attacks' by anyone with access to the original encoder. Privacy risk is evaluated empirically in the random-unlearning experiments using Robust Membership Inference Attack (RMIA) accuracy (Shokri et al., 2017; Zarifzadeh et al., 2023) as a proxy, where a score near 0.5 (and near the Retraining baseline) indicates successful forgetting; this is an evaluation metric borrowed from existing literature, not a novel attack contributed by the paper.
limitations: The authors state three limitations explicitly in Section 5: (1) the method operates under a black-box/API-access threat model only -- encoder weights are unmodified, so the raw representation Z still leaks forget-set information and is vulnerable to white-box recovery attacks by anyone with access to the original encoder; (2) the inserted transformation f_phi imposes a permanent per-inference computational overhead (claimed negligible at microsecond scale, but persistent rather than one-time like retraining); (3) the zero-shot variant depends on the Neural Collapse hypothesis holding for the pretrained representation -- if class features have not sufficiently collapsed, the classifier-weight proxies for class centroids become inaccurate, imposing an intrinsic upper bound on zero-shot retain utility (elaborated in Appendix G, not extracted here). The paper also flags in Future Directions that its evaluation is restricted to image classification (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny ImageNet) and does not evaluate on LLMs or other modalities, proposing this as future work rather than a result of the current paper.

quantitative_results: Class unlearning CIFAR-10: Rep. Unl. Test Ar 93.5, Test Af 1.9, Test CE 0.36 vs. Retraining Ar 94.1/Af 0.0/CE 0.00
Class unlearning CIFAR-100: Rep. Unl. Test Ar 73.4, Test Af 0.2, Test CE 0.88 vs. Retraining Ar 74.4/Af 1.1/CE 0.00; Bad Teacher only 67.2% Ar
Class unlearning Tiny ImageNet: Rep. Unl. Test Ar 58.7, Test Af 0.6, Test CE 1.80 vs. Retraining Ar 59.7/Af 0.0/CE 0.00; SCRUB only 48.9% Ar
Zero-shot class unlearning: Rep. Unl. ZS Ar 93.2/69.3/51.6 on CIFAR-10/100/Tiny ImageNet vs. EMMN collapsing to Ar 23.3/1.2/0.5
Random unlearning: Rep. Unl. Train Ar 100.0/99.9/100.0, RMIA 57.7/70.9/75.6, Test CE 0.14/0.69/1.13 across CIFAR-10/100/Tiny ImageNet, vs. Retraining RMIA 54.2/68.7/73.0
Compute speed-up vs. full retraining on Tiny ImageNet: Rep. Unl. ZS 757x, Rep. Unl. 232.8x (Fig. 4c)
Compute speed-up on CIFAR-10: Rep. Unl. ZS 174.4x, Rep. Unl. 72.5x; on CIFAR-100: Rep. Unl. ZS 390.9x, Rep. Unl. 169.0x
Per-inference overhead of transformation layer: 0.59 microseconds (1-layer MLP, CIFAR-10), 1.09 microseconds (Tiny ImageNet)
